channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: After a Bitter Split, European Leaders Play Nice With Trump
item.description: A peace framework with Iran, and hope for cooperation with Ukraine, softened the tone on Tuesday at a Group of 7 gathering in France.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: World leaders including President Trump during a Group of 7 session on Tuesday at Évian-les-Bains, France.

item.title: Who Are the Leaders at the G7 Summit in France?
item.description: On a roll or against a wall, Group of 7 leaders bring sharply different agendas. The leaders of some other nations are also attending to press their own interests.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Leaders meeting during the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, in France, on Tuesday.

item.title: Can a Trump-Modi Meeting Reset U.S.-India Relations?
item.description: Despite mutual praise between the two leaders, there has been a series of setbacks to U.S. relations with India since they last met more than a year ago.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prime Minister Narendra Modi of India in Nice, France, on Sunday.

item.title: After U.S. Strike on Iranian School, Months Pass Without Answers
item.description: U.S. officials have not publicly acknowledged responsibility for the deaths or released a report on their findings from an investigation into the Feb. 28 strike.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health care workers holding photos of children killed by airstrikes on the Shajarah Tayyebeh elementary school in Minab, Iran. At least 175 people were killed, according to Iranian officials.

item.title: The Iran War Permanently Altered the Global Economy
item.description: The global order has been altered, and economies are unlikely to simply pick up where they left off before the U.S. and Israel began bombing Iran.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Fishermen repairing a boat in the port of Tyre in southern Lebanon. The war with Iran has shaken trust in the region’s peace, stability and prosperity.

item.title: Republicans Again Block War Powers Measure in the Senate
item.description: Four G.O.P. senators crossed party lines to back the resolution to direct President Trump to halt the war in Iran and seek authorization from Congress, but their support was not enough.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Michael A. McCoy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The vote on Tuesday reflected a determination among Democrats to continue aggressively pushing back on President Trump’s handling of the war with Iran.

item.title: Six Takeaways From the Times Investigation Into Epstein’s Death
item.description: We considered every plausible theory of his death, both official and otherwise, seeking out the most persuasive arguments and evidence for each.
item.dc:creator: Charles Homans, Steve Eder, Jan Ransom and Michael Rothfeld
item.media:credit: Andrew Moore for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Metropolitan Correctional Center in Lower Manhattan, where Jeffrey Epstein arrived on the evening of July 6, 2019, and died 35 days later.

item.title: The Big Questions About Jeffrey Epstein: What The Times Has Learned
item.description: Here is what we know about the sexual predator whose secrets spurred an international reckoning over money, power and complicity. Check back for updates.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: Why Is Everyone Waiting Hours for Frozen Yogurt? Why Am I?
item.description: The yogurt is not special. Let’s just get that out of the way now.
item.dc:creator: Amy X. Wang

item.title: Bay Area Voters to Decide on Swalwell Replacement
item.description: Eric Swalwell resigned in April as he faced sexual assault allegations, prompting a special election to fill out his term. Two Democrats topped the field in a regular primary election this month.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla

item.title: Hillary Clinton Says Biden’s Re-election Bid Was a ‘Terrible Mistake’
item.description: If the former president had “passed the torch” and allowed a competitive Democratic primary in 2024, she said in a new interview, the winner “would have beaten Donald Trump.”
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:description: Hillary Clinton and former President Joseph R. Biden Jr. have long maintained a relationship that is warm in public but intensely competitive and even resentful in private.

item.title: Democrats Keep Helping Out This Far-Right Maryland Republican
item.description: Gov. Wes Moore, a Democrat, has begun running ads on Fox News that promote Dan Cox, a Republican running in a contested primary to challenge him.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein

item.title: Claiming an Antifa Plot, U.S. Charges 15 in Minneapolis With Conspiracy
item.description: The conspiracy indictment was filed as federal prosecutors in Minnesota had trouble sustaining many criminal cases filed against Immigration and Customs Enforcement protesters.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra, Alan Feuer and Jeff Ernst
item.media:credit: Alex Kormann/Minnesota Star Tribune, via Associated Press
item.media:description: United States Attorney Daniel N. Rosen spoke at a news conference in Minneapolis this month.

item.title: Judge Orders Kennedy Center to Make a Plan for Staying Open
item.description: As the Trump administration seeks to move forward with renovations at the center, a judge has asked for its programming calendar.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: The John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts has three days to update a federal judge on whether it still intends to push for a two-year closure for renovations, a decision he has temporarily blocked.

item.title: Trump Breaks Up Education Dept., Prompting Worries Over Civil Rights
item.description: Special education programs and the civil rights office will be moved out of the Education Department, the most aggressive move yet by the Trump administration to dismantle the agency.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender and Dana Goldstein
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Education Department will shift duties for its Office for Civil Rights, which for decades has enforced anti-discrimination laws related to school children, to the Justice Department.

item.title: At Least 5 Charged in Alleged Plot to Attack White House During U.F.C. Event
item.description: Charging documents outlined an ambitious plan involving explosive-laden drones and rifles, but left less clear that the conspirators had the means to carry it out.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:description: Ultimate Fighting Championship matches taking place on the White House lawn on Sunday.

item.title: Takeaways From JD Vance’s New Book ‘Communion,’ on Faith and His Political Ambitions
item.description: In a new memoir, Mr. Vance recalls an “unsettling” meeting with Vatican officials on immigration policy and disavows his infamous disparagement of some Democrats as “childless cat ladies.”
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard for The New York Times
item.media:description: In his new memoir, “Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith,” Vice President JD Vance makes a running start on defining his political philosophy as the 2028 presidential race starts to take shape.

item.title: Vance Is Pressed on Epstein, Race and His Boss on ‘The View’
item.description: The vice president also talked to Megyn Kelly on Tuesday about critics of the new Iran cease-fire and his presidential ambition.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Communion: Finding My Way Back to Faith (Book)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance’s book tour coincides with the administration’s drawdown of the conflict with Iran.

item.title: Ebola Outbreak Could Become Worst on Record, Africa C.D.C. Chief Warns
item.description: Health workers have warned that the outbreak, already one of the worst in decades, could take as long as a year to contain if infection rates do not flatten.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Treating a patient suspected of having Ebola at a hospital in Mongbwalu, Democratic Republic of Congo, last month.

item.title: SpaceX Is More Valuable Than Meta, Broadcom and Many Others
item.description: In its first few days of trading, Elon Musk’s newly listed rocket maker has become one of the world’s most valuable companies.
item.dc:creator: Jason Karaian

item.title: D.O.J. Seeks to Halt Pollution Lawsuit Against Elon Musk’s Data Center
item.description: The department cited national security concerns, saying Elon Musk’s company had played a crucial role in the Iran war. It also argued it has the authority to stop environmental lawsuits brought by citizens.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Construction at xAI’s power plant in Southaven, Miss., in February. The NAACP has sued the company over air pollution from generators there.

item.title: These Dating Apps Want You to Meet at the Gym
item.description: A slew of new apps focus on connecting people who love to work out.
item.dc:creator: Danielle Friedman

item.title: Are You a Morning Person or Night Owl? Take Our Sleep Quiz.
item.description: Think you’re a morning person, a night owl or something in between? Take our “chronotype” quiz to find out.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Schupak and Montse Galbany

item.title: Iran Found Trump’s Bone Spur
item.description: In a contest of wills, the hard men of Tehran prevailed over the vain man of Washington.
item.dc:creator: Bret Stephens
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Tam Stockton for The New York Times

item.title: Good Luck, Kevin Warsh. You’ll Need It.
item.description: Between a surge in interest rates, increasingly restive colleagues and the demands of President Trump, the new Fed chairman, Kevin Warsh, is in a bind.
item.dc:creator: Steven Rattner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Tell Us Why Taylor Swift’s Wedding Is Meaningful to You
item.description: For an upcoming story, we want fans to talk to us about the journey they have been on with Swift through the years and how they feel about her big day.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce will be tying the knot in the near future.

item.title: Federal Judge Blocks Idaho Law Criminalizing Transgender Bathroom Use
item.description: Six transgender residents claimed that the bathroom ban, the most restrictive in the nation, violated their constitutional rights.
item.dc:creator: Amy Harmon
